---
title: "References & Further Reading"
subtitle: "Curated resources for every chapter — textbooks, papers, courses, and tools"
date: last-modified
toc: true
toc-depth: 3
toc-title: "Jump To"
number-sections: false
---

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
Resources are organized to match the tutorial chapters. Each entry is annotated with its **difficulty level**, **format**, and **why it matters**.
:::

---

## 📚 Foundational Textbooks

These are the canonical references for the entire tutorial. Keep them bookmarked.

| Book | Authors | Year | Level | Best For |
|------|---------|------|-------|----------|
| *Pattern Recognition and Machine Learning* | Bishop | 2006 | ★★★ Intermediate–Advanced | Deep probabilistic foundations |
| *An Introduction to Statistical Learning* (ISLR) | James, Witten, Hastie, Tibshirani | 2021 | ★★ Beginner–Intermediate | Conceptual clarity, R/Python labs |
| *The Elements of Statistical Learning* (ESL) | Hastie, Tibshirani, Friedman | 2009 | ★★★ Advanced | Rigorous mathematical treatment |
| *Deep Learning* | Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville | 2016 | ★★★ Advanced | Neural networks, backpropagation, generative models |
| *Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach* | Russell & Norvig | 2020 | ★★ Intermediate | Broad AI context including search, planning, RL |
| *Mathematics for Machine Learning* | Deisenroth, Faisal, Ong | 2020 | ★★ Intermediate | Linear algebra, calculus, probability for ML |
| *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction* | Sutton & Barto | 2018 | ★★★ Advanced | Definitive RL textbook |
| *Hands-On Machine Learning* | Géron | 2022 | ★ Beginner | Practical, scikit-learn / TensorFlow |

::: {.callout-note}
**Free downloads**: ISLR, ESL, Deep Learning, Mathematics for ML, and Sutton & Barto are all freely available as PDFs from the authors' official websites.
:::

---

## 🧮 Mathematical Prerequisites

*Relevant to: Chapter 00 — Prerequisites & Setup*

### Linear Algebra

- **3Blue1Brown — Essence of Linear Algebra** (YouTube series, 2016)  
  *Visual, intuitive introduction to vectors, matrices, and transformations. Best 3 hours you'll spend on linear algebra.*  
  → [youtube.com/playlist?list=PLZHQObOWTQDPD3MizzM2xVFitgF8hE_ab](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLZHQObOWTQDPD3MizzM2xVFitgF8hE_ab)

- **Gilbert Strang — Linear Algebra (MIT 18.06)** (Lecture series)  
  *The gold standard university course. Covers everything from dot products to SVD.*  
  → [ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-06sc-linear-algebra-fall-2011](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-06sc-linear-algebra-fall-2011/)

- Deisenroth, M.P., Faisal, A.A., & Ong, C.S. (2020). *Mathematics for Machine Learning*, Ch. 2–4. Cambridge University Press.

### Calculus & Optimization

- **3Blue1Brown — Essence of Calculus** (YouTube series, 2017)  
  *Builds intuition for derivatives, chain rule, and gradients — exactly what you need for backpropagation.*  
  → [youtube.com/playlist?list=PLZHQObOWTQDMsr9K-rj53DwVRMYO3t5Yr](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLZHQObOWTQDMsr9K-rj53DwVRMYO3t5Yr)

- Nocedal, J. & Wright, S. (2006). *Numerical Optimization* (2nd ed.). Springer.  
  *Comprehensive reference on gradient descent, quasi-Newton methods, constrained optimization.*

### Probability & Statistics

- **Khan Academy — Statistics and Probability** (Free course)  
  *Covers all the basics: distributions, Bayes' theorem, expectation, variance.*  
  → [khanacademy.org/math/statistics-probability](https://www.khanacademy.org/math/statistics-probability)

- DeGroot, M.H. & Schervish, M.J. (2012). *Probability and Statistics* (4th ed.). Pearson.

---

## 🟢 Beginner Chapters

### Chapter 01 — Introduction to Machine Learning

- Mitchell, T. (1997). *Machine Learning*. McGraw-Hill.  
  *Classic textbook; Chapter 1 gives the cleanest definition of ML you'll find.*

- Domingos, P. (2012). A few useful things to know about machine learning. *Communications of the ACM*, 55(10), 78–87.  
  *Readable 9-page overview; covers the bias-variance tradeoff, overfitting, feature engineering.*

- **Andrew Ng — Machine Learning Specialization** (Coursera, 2022)  
  *Best entry-point structured course. Covers supervised, unsupervised, and RL across three courses.*  
  → [coursera.org/specializations/machine-learning-introduction](https://www.coursera.org/specializations/machine-learning-introduction)

### Chapter 02 — Linear Regression

- James et al. (2021). *ISLR*, Ch. 3: Linear Regression. *(Free PDF available)*  
  *Excellent chapter covering simple and multiple regression, subset selection, and regularization.*

- Hastie et al. (2009). *ESL*, Ch. 3: Linear Methods for Regression.  
  *More rigorous treatment including geometric interpretation and the hat matrix.*

- Ng, A. (2022). *CS229 Lecture Notes: Linear Regression and Gradient Descent*.  
  → [cs229.stanford.edu/notes2022fall/main_notes.pdf](https://cs229.stanford.edu/notes2022fall/main_notes.pdf)

- Gauss, C.F. (1809). *Theoria Motus Corporum Coelestium*. *(Historical)*  
  *The original least-squares derivation — over 200 years old and still the foundation.*

### Chapter 03 — Logistic Regression

- Cox, D.R. (1958). The regression analysis of binary sequences. *Journal of the Royal Statistical Society*, Series B, 20(2), 215–232.  
  *The original logistic regression paper.*

- James et al. (2021). *ISLR*, Ch. 4: Classification. *(Free PDF)*  
  *Clear coverage of logistic regression, LDA, and decision boundaries.*

- Ng, A. (2022). *CS229 Lecture Notes: Classification and Logistic Regression*.  
  *Derives the log-likelihood and gradient update from first principles.*

- **StatQuest: Logistic Regression** (YouTube, Josh Starmer)  
  *Clear visual explanation of odds, log-odds, and the sigmoid function.*  
  → [youtu.be/yIYKR4sgzI8](https://youtu.be/yIYKR4sgzI8)

### Chapter 04 — K-Nearest Neighbors

- Cover, T. & Hart, P. (1967). Nearest neighbor pattern classification. *IEEE Transactions on Information Theory*, 13(1), 21–27.  
  *The foundational KNN paper. Proves that KNN error rate is at most twice the Bayes error.*

- Fix, E. & Hodges, J.L. (1951). *Discriminatory Analysis, Nonparametric Discrimination: Consistency Properties*. USAF School of Aviation Medicine Technical Report.  
  *The original unpublished report that introduced the KNN concept.*

- Bellman, R. (1961). *Adaptive Control Processes: A Guided Tour*. Princeton University Press.  
  *Where the term "curse of dimensionality" was coined.*

- James et al. (2021). *ISLR*, Ch. 2.2.3: K-Nearest Neighbors.

### Chapter 05 — Naive Bayes

- Bayes, T. (1763). An essay towards solving a problem in the doctrine of chances. *Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society*, 53, 370–418. *(Historical)*  
  *The original Bayes' theorem paper, published posthumously.*

- McCallum, A. & Nigam, K. (1998). A comparison of event models for Naive Bayes text classification. *AAAI Workshop on Learning for Text Categorization*.  
  *Compares Bernoulli NB vs Multinomial NB for document classification — highly cited.*

- Zhang, H. (2004). The optimality of Naive Bayes. *FLAIRS Conference*.  
  *Explains why the "naive" independence assumption works surprisingly well in practice.*

- Manning, C.D., Raghavan, P., & Schütze, H. (2008). *Introduction to Information Retrieval*, Ch. 13. Cambridge University Press.  
  *Excellent treatment of NB for text; freely available online.*

---

## 🟡 Intermediate Chapters

### Chapter 06 — Decision Trees

- Breiman, L., Friedman, J., Olshen, R., & Stone, C. (1984). *Classification and Regression Trees*. Wadsworth.  
  *The original CART book — introduced Gini impurity and the cost-complexity pruning algorithm.*

- Quinlan, J.R. (1986). Induction of decision trees. *Machine Learning*, 1(1), 81–106.  
  *Introduced the ID3 algorithm using information gain. Foundational.*

- Quinlan, J.R. (1993). *C4.5: Programs for Machine Learning*. Morgan Kaufmann.  
  *Extension of ID3 with continuous features, pruning, and missing values.*

- James et al. (2021). *ISLR*, Ch. 8: Tree-Based Methods.  
  *Accessible coverage with comparison to linear models.*

- **StatQuest: Decision Trees** (YouTube)  
  → [youtu.be/_L39rN6gz7Y](https://youtu.be/_L39rN6gz7Y)

### Chapter 07 — Support Vector Machines

- Cortes, C. & Vapnik, V. (1995). Support-vector networks. *Machine Learning*, 20(3), 273–297.  
  *The original SVM paper introducing the soft-margin formulation.*

- Boser, B.E., Guyon, I.M., & Vapnik, V.N. (1992). A training algorithm for optimal margin classifiers. *COLT*, 144–152.  
  *Introduced the kernel trick for non-linear SVMs.*

- Schölkopf, B. & Smola, A. (2002). *Learning with Kernels*. MIT Press.  
  *Comprehensive reference on kernel methods and the reproducing kernel Hilbert space framework.*

- Burges, C.J.C. (1998). A tutorial on support vector machines for pattern recognition. *Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery*, 2(2), 121–167.  
  *Accessible 46-page tutorial — best single introduction to the full SVM theory.*

- Chang, C.C. & Lin, C.J. (2011). LIBSVM: A library for support vector machines. *ACM TIST*, 2(3), 1–27.  
  *The widely-used SVM implementation; the paper explains SMO algorithm details.*

### Chapter 08 — Ensemble Methods

- Breiman, L. (1996). Bagging predictors. *Machine Learning*, 24(2), 123–140.  
  *Introduced bootstrap aggregating — the core idea behind Random Forests.*

- Breiman, L. (2001). Random forests. *Machine Learning*, 45(1), 5–32.  
  *The definitive Random Forest paper. One of the most cited ML papers of all time.*

- Freund, Y. & Schapire, R.E. (1997). A decision-theoretic generalization of on-line learning and an application to boosting. *Journal of Computer and System Sciences*, 55(1), 119–139.  
  *The original AdaBoost paper. Introduced the idea of adaptively reweighting samples.*

- Friedman, J.H. (2001). Greedy function approximation: A gradient boosting machine. *Annals of Statistics*, 29(5), 1189–1232.  
  *Derived gradient boosting from a functional gradient descent perspective.*

- Chen, T. & Guestrin, C. (2016). XGBoost: A scalable tree boosting system. *KDD*, 785–794.  
  *The paper behind the competition-winning XGBoost library — explains regularized trees.*

---

## 🔴 Advanced Chapters

### Chapter 09 — Neural Networks (MLP)

- Rosenblatt, F. (1958). The perceptron: A probabilistic model for information storage and organization in the brain. *Psychological Review*, 65(6), 386–408.  
  *Introduced the perceptron — the first trainable single-layer neural network.*

- Rumelhart, D.E., Hinton, G.E., & Williams, R.J. (1986). Learning representations by back-propagating errors. *Nature*, 323, 533–536.  
  *Popularized backpropagation — the algorithm that made deep learning possible.*

- Hornik, K., Stinchcombe, M., & White, H. (1989). Multilayer feedforward networks are universal approximators. *Neural Networks*, 2(5), 359–366.  
  *Proves that a single hidden layer can approximate any continuous function — the Universal Approximation Theorem.*

- LeCun, Y., Bengio, Y., & Hinton, G. (2015). Deep learning. *Nature*, 521, 436–444.  
  *Accessible 9-page overview of the deep learning revolution by three of its founders.*

- He, K., Zhang, X., Ren, S., & Sun, J. (2015). Delving deep into rectifiers: Surpassing human-level performance on ImageNet classification. *ICCV*, 1026–1034.  
  *Introduced He (Kaiming) initialization — the weight init method used in this tutorial.*

- **3Blue1Brown — Neural Networks** (YouTube series, 2017)  
  *The most-watched neural network explainer. Covers forward pass, gradient descent, and backpropagation visually.*  
  → [youtube.com/playlist?list=PLZHQObOWTQDNU6R1_67000Dx_ZCJB-3pi](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLZHQObOWTQDNU6R1_67000Dx_ZCJB-3pi)

- Goodfellow et al. (2016). *Deep Learning*, Ch. 6–8. *(Free PDF)*  
  *The definitive deep learning textbook chapters on feedforward networks, regularization, and optimization.*

### Chapter 10 — Convolutional Neural Networks

- LeCun, Y., Bottou, L., Bengio, Y., & Haffner, P. (1998). Gradient-based learning applied to document recognition. *Proceedings of the IEEE*, 86(11), 2278–2324.  
  *Introduced LeNet-5 — the first successful CNN for digit recognition on MNIST.*

- Krizhevsky, A., Sutskever, I., & Hinton, G.E. (2012). ImageNet classification with deep convolutional neural networks. *NeurIPS*, 1097–1105.  
  *AlexNet — the paper that launched the modern deep learning era by winning ImageNet 2012.*

- Simonyan, K. & Zisserman, A. (2015). Very deep convolutional networks for large-scale image recognition. *ICLR*. (VGGNet)  
  *Showed that network depth using small 3×3 kernels is a critical component for accuracy.*

- He, K., Zhang, X., Ren, S., & Sun, J. (2016). Deep residual learning for image recognition. *CVPR*, 770–778.  
  *Introduced skip connections (ResNet) solving the vanishing gradient problem in very deep networks.*

- Goodfellow et al. (2016). *Deep Learning*, Ch. 9: Convolutional Networks. *(Free PDF)*

- **CS231n: Convolutional Neural Networks for Visual Recognition** (Stanford)  
  *The best-structured CNN course. All notes, slides, and assignments free online.*  
  → [cs231n.stanford.edu](http://cs231n.stanford.edu)

### Chapter 11 — Recurrent Neural Networks & LSTM

- Elman, J.L. (1990). Finding structure in time. *Cognitive Science*, 14(2), 179–211.  
  *Introduced the basic Elman RNN architecture with recurrent hidden states.*

- Hochreiter, S. & Schmidhuber, J. (1997). Long short-term memory. *Neural Computation*, 9(8), 1735–1780.  
  *The LSTM paper — one of the most cited papers in all of machine learning.*

- Hochreiter, S. (1991). *Untersuchungen zu dynamischen neuronalen Netzen* [Master's thesis, TU Munich].  
  *Where the vanishing gradient problem was first formally analyzed.*

- Cho, K., van Merrienboer, B., Gulcehre, C., Bahdanau, D., Bougares, F., Schwenk, H., & Bengio, Y. (2014). Learning phrase representations using RNN encoder-decoder for statistical machine translation. *EMNLP*, 1724–1734.  
  *Introduced the GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) as a simpler alternative to LSTM.*

- Graves, A. (2012). *Supervised Sequence Labelling with Recurrent Neural Networks*. Springer.  
  *Comprehensive treatment of sequence learning with RNNs.*

- Karpathy, A. (2015). The unreasonable effectiveness of recurrent neural networks. *Blog post.*  
  *Legendary blog post demonstrating character-level RNNs on Shakespeare, Linux kernel, LaTeX.*  
  → [karpathy.github.io/2015/05/21/rnn-effectiveness](https://karpathy.github.io/2015/05/21/rnn-effectiveness/)

### Chapter 14 — Reinforcement Learning

- Sutton, R.S. & Barto, A.G. (2018). *Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction* (2nd ed.). MIT Press. *(Free PDF)*  
  *The definitive RL textbook. Covers everything from bandits to TD learning to policy gradients.*  
  → [incompleteideas.net/book/the-book-2nd.html](http://incompleteideas.net/book/the-book-2nd.html)

- Watkins, C.J.C.H. & Dayan, P. (1992). Q-learning. *Machine Learning*, 8(3–4), 279–292.  
  *The original Q-learning convergence proof paper.*

- Bellman, R. (1957). *Dynamic Programming*. Princeton University Press.  
  *Introduced the Bellman equation — the recursive optimality condition underlying all DP and RL.*

- Mnih, V. et al. (2015). Human-level control through deep reinforcement learning. *Nature*, 518, 529–533.  
  *The DQN paper from DeepMind — combined Q-learning with CNNs to play Atari games at human level.*

- Silver, D. et al. (2016). Mastering the game of Go with deep neural networks and tree search. *Nature*, 529, 484–489.  
  *AlphaGo — milestone paper combining deep RL with Monte Carlo Tree Search.*

- **DeepMind x UCL Lectures on Reinforcement Learning** (YouTube, 2021)  
  *12-lecture series by DeepMind researchers. Rigorous and modern.*  
  → [youtube.com/playlist?list=PLqYmG7hTraZDVH599EItlEWsUOsJbAodm](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLqYmG7hTraZDVH599EItlEWsUOsJbAodm)

---

## 🟡 Unsupervised Learning Chapters

### Chapter 12 — K-Means Clustering

- MacQueen, J. (1967). Some methods for classification and analysis of multivariate observations. *5th Berkeley Symposium on Mathematical Statistics and Probability*, 1, 281–297.  
  *The original K-Means paper.*

- Arthur, D. & Vassilvitskii, S. (2007). k-means++: The advantages of careful seeding. *SODA*, 1027–1035.  
  *Introduced K-Means++ initialization — guarantees O(log k) approximation to optimal solution.*

- Lloyd, S.P. (1982). Least squares quantization in PCM. *IEEE Transactions on Information Theory*, 28(2), 129–137.  
  *The "Lloyd's algorithm" paper — the standard iterative K-Means procedure.*

- Rousseeuw, P.J. (1987). Silhouettes: A graphical aid to the interpretation and validation of cluster analysis. *Journal of Computational and Applied Mathematics*, 20, 53–65.  
  *Introduced the silhouette score for cluster quality evaluation.*

- **StatQuest: K-means Clustering** (YouTube)  
  → [youtu.be/4b5d3muPQmA](https://youtu.be/4b5d3muPQmA)

### Chapter 13 — Principal Component Analysis

- Pearson, K. (1901). On lines and planes of closest fit to systems of points in space. *Philosophical Magazine*, 2(11), 559–572.  
  *The original PCA paper — over 120 years old.*

- Hotelling, H. (1933). Analysis of a complex of statistical variables into principal components. *Journal of Educational Psychology*, 24(6), 417–441.  
  *Re-derived PCA independently and gave it the modern "principal components" name.*

- Jolliffe, I.T. (2002). *Principal Component Analysis* (2nd ed.). Springer.  
  *The definitive monograph on PCA — covers every variant, application, and interpretation.*

- Shlens, J. (2014). A tutorial on principal component analysis. *arXiv:1404.1100*.  
  *Free, 12-page tutorial — very readable derivation from covariance matrices to eigenvectors.*  
  → [arxiv.org/abs/1404.1100](https://arxiv.org/abs/1404.1100)

- van der Maaten, L. & Hinton, G. (2008). Visualizing data using t-SNE. *JMLR*, 9, 2579–2605.  
  *t-SNE — the most popular non-linear alternative to PCA for visualization.*

---

## 📊 Model Evaluation (Chapter 15)

- Kohavi, R. (1995). A study of cross-validation and bootstrap for accuracy estimation and model selection. *IJCAI*, 1137–1143.  
  *Comprehensive comparison of hold-out, k-fold CV, and bootstrap for model selection.*

- Bradley, A.P. (1997). The use of the area under the ROC curve in the evaluation of machine learning algorithms. *Pattern Recognition*, 30(7), 1145–1159.  
  *Why AUC-ROC is a robust evaluation metric for classifiers.*

- Chawla, N.V., Bowyer, K.W., Hall, L.O., & Kegelmeyer, W.P. (2002). SMOTE: Synthetic minority over-sampling technique. *JAIR*, 16, 321–357.  
  *Addresses class imbalance — highly relevant when accuracy is misleading.*

- Tibshirani, R. (1996). Regression shrinkage and selection via the lasso. *JRSS-B*, 58(1), 267–288.  
  *Introduced Lasso (L1) regularization — fundamental for sparse models and feature selection.*

- Hoerl, A.E. & Kennard, R.W. (1970). Ridge regression: Biased estimation for nonorthogonal problems. *Technometrics*, 12(1), 55–67.  
  *The original Ridge (L2) regularization paper.*

- Bergstra, J. & Bengio, Y. (2012). Random search for hyper-parameter optimization. *JMLR*, 13, 281–305.  
  *Shows random search is often as good or better than grid search for hyperparameter tuning.*

---

## 🌐 Free Online Courses

| Course | Provider | Level | Topics |
|--------|----------|-------|--------|
| [Machine Learning Specialization](https://www.coursera.org/specializations/machine-learning-introduction) | Coursera / Andrew Ng | ★ Beginner | Supervised, unsupervised, RL |
| [Deep Learning Specialization](https://www.coursera.org/specializations/deep-learning) | Coursera / Andrew Ng | ★★ Intermediate | NN, CNN, RNN, attention |
| [CS229: Machine Learning](https://cs229.stanford.edu) | Stanford | ★★★ Advanced | Full ML theory |
| [CS231n: CNNs for Visual Recognition](http://cs231n.stanford.edu) | Stanford | ★★★ Advanced | Computer vision |
| [CS224n: NLP with Deep Learning](http://web.stanford.edu/class/cs224n) | Stanford | ★★★ Advanced | NLP, transformers |
| [fast.ai Practical Deep Learning](https://course.fast.ai) | fast.ai | ★ Beginner | Top-down, code-first DL |
| [Reinforcement Learning Lectures](https://www.davidsilver.uk/teaching/) | David Silver (DeepMind) | ★★★ Advanced | Full RL theory |
| [Mathematics for ML](https://www.coursera.org/specializations/mathematics-machine-learning) | Coursera / Imperial | ★★ Intermediate | Linear algebra, calculus, PCA |

---

## 🛠️ Python Ecosystem

### Standard Library (used in this tutorial)

| Module | Key Functions Used | Purpose in ML |
|--------|-------------------|---------------|
| `math` | `sqrt`, `log`, `exp`, `tanh` | Activation functions, loss computation |
| `random` | `random`, `gauss`, `shuffle`, `sample` | Initialization, stochastic gradient descent |
| `collections` | `Counter`, `defaultdict` | Vote counting in trees, KNN |
| `itertools` | `product` | Grid search over hyperparameters |

### Scientific Python Stack (for extending this tutorial)

| Library | Version | Role |
|---------|---------|------|
| [NumPy](https://numpy.org) | 1.26+ | Efficient array operations |
| [pandas](https://pandas.pydata.org) | 2.0+ | Data loading and preprocessing |
| [matplotlib](https://matplotlib.org) | 3.8+ | Visualization |
| [scikit-learn](https://scikit-learn.org) | 1.4+ | Reference implementations to compare against |
| [PyTorch](https://pytorch.org) | 2.0+ | Deep learning framework |
| [Quarto](https://quarto.org) | 1.6+ | This website |

---

## 📰 Landmark Papers Timeline

A chronological map of the key ideas in this tutorial:

```
1763  Bayes' theorem (Thomas Bayes)
1901  PCA introduced (Karl Pearson)
1957  Dynamic Programming / Bellman equation (Richard Bellman)
1958  Perceptron (Frank Rosenblatt)
1967  K-Means clustering (James MacQueen)
1984  CART — Decision Trees (Breiman, Friedman et al.)
1986  Backpropagation popularized (Rumelhart, Hinton, Williams)
1992  Q-Learning convergence proof (Watkins & Dayan)
1995  Support Vector Machines (Cortes & Vapnik)
1996  Bagging (Leo Breiman)
1997  AdaBoost (Freund & Schapire)
1997  LSTM (Hochreiter & Schmidhuber)
1998  LeNet-5 / Convolutional Networks (LeCun et al.)
2001  Random Forests (Leo Breiman)
2001  Gradient Boosting (Jerome Friedman)
2006  Deep Belief Networks — deep learning revival (Hinton et al.)
2012  AlexNet — deep learning breakthrough (Krizhevsky et al.)
2014  GRU (Cho et al.) / GANs (Goodfellow et al.)
2015  ResNet — skip connections (He et al.)
2015  DQN — deep RL for Atari (Mnih et al.)
2017  Transformers / Attention Is All You Need (Vaswani et al.)
2018  BERT (Devlin et al.) / GPT (OpenAI)
2020  GPT-3 (OpenAI)
2022  ChatGPT / Instruction-tuned LLMs
```

---

## 🔗 Useful Websites & Blogs

| Resource | URL | Why It's Useful |
|----------|-----|----------------|
| **Distill.pub** | [distill.pub](https://distill.pub) | Interactive, beautifully explained ML articles |
| **Towards Data Science** | [towardsdatascience.com](https://towardsdatascience.com) | Practical ML tutorials |
| **Papers With Code** | [paperswithcode.com](https://paperswithcode.com) | State-of-the-art benchmarks + code |
| **arXiv cs.LG** | [arxiv.org/list/cs.LG](https://arxiv.org/list/cs.LG) | Latest ML research preprints |
| **StatQuest** | [statquest.org](https://statquest.org) | YouTube channel: best visual ML explanations |
| **Andrej Karpathy's Blog** | [karpathy.github.io](https://karpathy.github.io) | Deep intuition from one of the field's best |
| **Chris Olah's Blog** | [colah.github.io](https://colah.github.io) | Legendary posts on neural nets, LSTMs, attention |
| **Lilian Weng's Blog** | [lilianweng.github.io](https://lilianweng.github.io) | Deep technical surveys on RL, NLP, diffusion models |

---

*Last updated: 2026-05-23*
